text: The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to carefully pull down the omentum protruding into the defect of an indirect inguinal hernia on the left side. An assistant aids by pushing the hernia content from above to facilitate reduction.

The surgeon has removed all hernia contents and is preparing to place a 15x15 cm mesh over the defect. The mesh is positioned with its center aligned to the center of the defect, ensuring a 6 cm margin around the healthy tissue. The surgeon begins securing the mesh using a tacker.

The surgeon uses an absorbable polygalactane tacker to secure the mesh around the hernia repair site, avoiding critical areas such as the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster. The mesh is positioned to be fired at least ten centimeters above the upper border of the pubic symphysis, taking care to avoid the bladder and inferior epigastric vessels. The tacker is applied using a supraumbilical port, facilitating fixation on the rectus abdominis and Cooper ligament, with no visible bleeding during the procedure.

The surgeon uses an absorbable tacker to secure the Y-Pro-II mesh, ensuring it is anchored two centimeters all around the defect. The left hand opposes the abdominal wall to maintain perpendicularity of the tacker tip for optimal mesh fixation. The mesh is fixed over the rectus abdominis muscle and Cooper's ligament, avoiding areas like the trapezoid of disaster, triangle of doom, and triangle of pain.

The surgeon uses a Dundee jamming knot to secure the inferomedial edge of the mesh, carefully pulling the peritoneum to avoid pricking it. The knot is locked by passing the needle through the loop, ensuring the mesh is fixed to prevent recurrence. The procedure continues with continuous suturing and Aberdeen termination.

The surgeon carefully lifts the peritoneum and positions it near the tip of the needle to avoid deep penetration. Locking sutures are applied to ensure the mesh is completely sealed at the lower edge, preventing bowel from lifting the mesh and entering the defect. The technique avoids pricking the peritoneum, reducing the risk of injuring the external iliac vein or artery.

The surgeon interlocks sutures to reinforce the mesh placement, carefully lifting the peritoneum over the needle to avoid damaging the external iliac vein and artery. The procedure progresses past the triangle of doom, with caution exercised around the spermatic vessels to prevent injury.

The surgeon carefully locks the sutures to press the mesh against the peritoneum, ensuring it is tight to prevent any hematoma formation. The mesh is gradually absorbed over time, and continuous suturing is performed to secure the lower edge, resembling the muffing technique used in tailoring. Elastic bandages are applied post-surgery to support the scrotum and prevent dead space, minimizing swelling.

The surgeon completes the interlocking sutures around the inferior surface of the hernia repair site, using a 40-centimeter suture for better coverage. A termination knot is placed on healthy tissue, employing a loop within a loop technique to secure the suture. The needle is then removed after locking the non-needle end.

The surgeon completes the aberrant termination of the suture in the inguinal hernia repair using the IPAM technique. The lower edge is visibly adhered to the mesh, ensuring the defect area is closed without allowing any omentum or bubbles to enter. The needle is removed, marking the end of the procedure.
